name: Python LISTS
description: Questions on Python lists: access, change, add, remove, loop, sort, copy, join, and slicing.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of appending to a Python list?
questions.explanation: Python lists use dynamic arrays. Append is O(1) amortized because the array doubles in size when full.

questions.question: What does my_list[::-1] return?
questions.explanation: The slice [::-1] creates a reversed shallow copy of the list by stepping backwards.

questions.question: Which creates a new list without the element at index i?
questions.explanation: Slicing lst[:i] + lst[i+1:] builds a new list without mutating the original. pop and remove mutate; del removes in place.

questions.question: What is the result of [1, 2, 3] * 2?
questions.explanation: Multiplying a list by an integer repeats the list that many times, producing a shallow copy of references.

questions.question: What is the default value of the second argument in list.sort()?
questions.explanation: list.sort(key=None, reverse=False). key is the function used for comparison; None means natural order.

questions.question: Which is O(1) for a list?
questions.explanation: append is amortized O(1). insert(0,x) and remove are O(n); 'in' is O(n) for lists.

questions.question: What does lst[-1] return?
questions.explanation: Negative indices count from the end. -1 is the last element, -2 the second-to-last, etc.

questions.question: If lst has length 5, what happens when you access lst[10]?
questions.explanation: Accessing an index >= len(lst) raises IndexError. Use valid indices 0 through len(lst)-1 (or negative -1 through -len(lst)).

questions.question: How do you change the element at index 2 in a list?
questions.explanation: Lists are mutable; use index assignment: lst[i] = value. This modifies the list in place.

questions.question: What does lst.extend([2, 3]) do?
choices: Adds the list [2,3] as a single element
Adds 2 and 3 as two elements to the end
questions.explanation: extend(iterable) appends each item from the iterable. So extend([2,3]) adds two elements; append([2,3]) would add one nested list.

questions.question: lst.append([1,2]) vs lst.extend([1,2]): what is the difference?
choices: append adds one element (a list), extend adds two elements
append adds two elements, extend adds one
questions.explanation: append(x) adds x as a single element. extend(iterable) adds each item from the iterable. So append([1,2]) gives [..., [1,2]]; extend([1,2]) gives [..., 1, 2].

questions.question: What does lst.pop() do (with no arguments)?
choices: Removes and returns the last element
Returns the last element without removing it
questions.explanation: pop() with no argument removes and returns the last element. pop(i) removes and returns the element at index i. Both mutate the list.

questions.question: What does lst.remove(x) do?
choices: Removes the element at index x
Removes the first occurrence of value x
Removes the last occurrence of x
questions.explanation: remove(x) removes the first item whose value equals x. It raises ValueError if x is not in the list. Use del lst[i] to remove by index.

questions.question: In for i, x in enumerate(lst): what do i and x represent?
choices: i is the value, x is the index
i is the index, x is the value
questions.explanation: enumerate(iterable) yields (index, value) pairs. So i is the 0-based index and x is the element at that index.

questions.question: What does sorted(lst) return?
questions.explanation: sorted(lst) returns a new list; it does not modify lst. lst.sort() sorts in place and returns None.

questions.question: What does lst.sort() return?
questions.explanation: list.sort() sorts the list in place and returns None. Use sorted(lst) if you need a new sorted list and want to keep the original.

questions.question: lst2 = lst[:] creates what?
choices: A reference to the same list
questions.explanation: lst[:] is a shallow copy: a new list with the same elements. If elements are mutable (e.g. nested lists), those inner objects are shared. Use copy.deepcopy for full independence.

questions.question: When do you need copy.deepcopy for a list?
choices: When the list contains mutable objects (e.g. nested lists)
questions.explanation: A shallow copy shares nested mutable objects. Changing a nested list in the copy affects the original. deepcopy creates independent copies of every nested object.

questions.question: What does " ".join(["a", "b", "c"]) return?
choices: Error: join is a list method
questions.explanation: str.join(iterable) joins the strings in the iterable with the separator. So " ".join(["a","b","c"]) gives "a b c". The separator can be "", "-", etc.

questions.question: For lst = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], what is lst[1:4]?
questions.explanation: Slicing [start:stop] includes start and excludes stop. lst[1:4] gives elements at indices 1, 2, 3 — the values 2, 3, 4.
